question_text: A team is building a real-time collaborative document editor and is debating between WebSockets and Server-Sent Events (SSE) for the sync layer. The app requires bidirectional updates (user A sees user B's cursor). Which is the right choice and why?
rationale: Clear architectural decision: SSE is unidirectional (server→client only), WebSockets are bidirectional. For real-time collaboration with cursor sync, WebSockets are the only viable choice. Architect should give a clear HEALTHY answer on WebSockets.

question_text: A startup is building a multi-tenant SaaS and debating between a single shared database (row-level tenant isolation) vs. a database-per-tenant approach. They have 50 tenants today and expect 5,000 in 3 years. Which pattern is appropriate for this scale?
rationale: Both patterns are viable depending on requirements. Single-DB scales cost-efficiently to 5K tenants but requires careful RLS. DB-per-tenant offers better isolation and simplicity but becomes operationally complex at 5K. Decision depends on compliance requirements, team ops maturity — genuinely trade-off-dependent.

question_text: A team is considering using a microservices architecture with 15 independent services for a product with 3 developers and $50K MRR. Is this the right architectural choice?
rationale: Classic premature microservices anti-pattern. 3 developers cannot effectively operate 15 services (deployment complexity, distributed tracing, service discovery, network overhead). A monolith or 2-3 service split is appropriate at this scale.

question_text: A system stores user sessions in a Redis cluster with TTL-based expiry. The team wants to add 'remember me' sessions that last 30 days. Is extending the TTL to 30 days the right implementation approach?
rationale: TTL extension works technically but creates security risk — a stolen session token is valid for 30 days. Better approach: sliding window TTL reset on activity, paired with refresh token rotation. Simple TTL extension is technically correct but architecturally naive for auth.

question_text: A team is building a vector search system and choosing between Qdrant, Pinecone, and pgvector. Their use case: 10M document embeddings, self-hosted, sub-50ms query latency. What is the right choice?
rationale: Qdrant is clearly the right choice: self-hosted (eliminates Pinecone), purpose-built for vector search (outperforms pgvector at 10M scale), HNSW indexing achieves sub-50ms at this scale. Clear architectural recommendation available.

question_text: A distributed system uses optimistic locking for concurrent updates to user account balances. Two users simultaneously withdraw $500 from a $600 balance. Will the optimistic locking prevent double-spend?
rationale: Optimistic locking with version checking prevents double-spend: both reads see version N, first write succeeds (version N+1), second write detects version conflict and is retried. Balance never goes negative if retry logic re-reads the current balance.

question_text: A team wants to use event sourcing as the primary data model for a simple CRUD user profile system. Is this an appropriate architecture choice?
rationale: Event sourcing is severely over-engineered for simple CRUD. It adds complexity (projections, event replay, eventual consistency) without benefit for use cases that don't require audit trails or temporal queries. CRUD on a relational DB is correct here.

question_text: A FastAPI application has 40+ endpoints all defined in a single main.py file (3,000+ lines). What is the right architectural intervention?
rationale: Split into APIRouter-based modules: routers/users.py, routers/payments.py, etc. Each router owns its domain. This is a standard FastAPI pattern with clear implementation. Unambiguous architectural recommendation — split by domain into routers.

question_text: A team is building an AI agent system and deciding between LangChain, a custom agent loop, and the Anthropic Claude Agent SDK. They need full control over tool execution, memory injection, and cost tracking. Which to choose?
rationale: LangChain adds abstraction overhead and hides cost tracking. Claude Agent SDK is purpose-built for Claude but newer with evolving API. Custom loop offers full control but requires more implementation. Choice depends on team velocity vs. control trade-off — genuinely contextual.

question_text: A team stores all application configuration in environment variables, including 200+ settings for a complex enterprise app. Is this the right configuration management pattern?
rationale: 12-factor app env vars are correct for secrets and deployment-specific config, but 200+ env vars indicates config sprawl. Structured config files (YAML/TOML) with env var overrides for secrets is the right pattern at this scale. Not a failure — common and functional, but has operational pain points.

question_text: A system needs to process 10,000 image uploads per hour. The team is debating between synchronous processing (resize + store in the API request handler) vs. async via a job queue. Which is architecturally correct?
rationale: Synchronous image processing in the API handler is architecturally wrong at this scale: it blocks request threads, creates request timeout risk, has no retry on failure, and prevents horizontal scaling of the API layer. Async job queue (Celery, BullMQ, etc.) is the only correct architecture.

question_text: A team is choosing between PostgreSQL and MongoDB for a new application that will store user profiles with dynamic fields that vary significantly per user type. Is MongoDB the right choice here?
rationale: Dynamic fields is a common MongoDB justification, but JSONB columns in PostgreSQL handle this case with full ACID guarantees and SQL querying. MongoDB is viable but not clearly superior unless the team already has MongoDB expertise or needs native document-level sharding. Context-dependent.

question_text: An API returns all user data including hashed passwords, internal IDs, and audit timestamps in every response, including the public profile endpoint. Is this an architectural problem?
rationale: Exposing hashed passwords in API responses is a critical security architecture failure — even bcrypt hashes can be cracked offline. Internal IDs and audit data leak implementation details. Response objects must be projection-scoped: public profiles never include auth data.

question_text: A Rust service processes financial transactions using async/await with Tokio. The team wants to add CPU-intensive fraud scoring (ML model inference) in the same async runtime. Will this cause performance problems?
rationale: CPU-bound work in an async runtime blocks the Tokio thread pool, starving I/O tasks and causing latency spikes. ML inference must run in a dedicated thread pool (tokio::task::spawn_blocking or rayon) to prevent async executor starvation.

question_text: A team uses a single JWT secret key for signing all user tokens across all environments (dev, staging, prod). Is this a security architecture problem?
rationale: Shared JWT secret across environments means a compromised dev secret allows forging production tokens. Each environment must have its own secret with separate key management. This is a critical security architecture failure.
